card.intuition: Beyond push/pop, heaps support decrease-key (update a priority) and arbitrary deletion — both require sifting up or down after the change. Efficient Dijkstra and Prim's use decrease-key to update shortest distances.
card.analogy: A hospital triage queue: patients can be re-prioritised when their condition changes (decrease-key). The patient with the highest priority always goes next. Updating a priority means re-sorting the queue from that patient's position.
card.time_complexity: O(log n) for push, pop, decrease-key, delete-at-index
when_to_use: Dijkstra / Prim's with decrease-key for O((V+E) log V)
A* search where node priorities are updated
Any priority queue that needs both extract-min and priority updates
gotchas: To find an element for decrease-key, maintain a position map: pos[key] = index in heap array
After decrease-key, always sift UP (new priority may violate parent invariant)
After increase-key (or delete), sift DOWN (new priority may violate children invariant)

exercise.prompt: Implement decrease-key on a min-heap with a position map.
hints: After decreasing the priority at index i, sift up from i — the smaller priority may now violate the parent's invariant
exercise.test_code:
h = IndexedMinHeap()
h.push('a', 10)
h.push('b', 5)
h.push('c', 8)
assert h.heap[0][1] == 'b'   # b has lowest priority
h.decrease_key('a', 2)        # a now has priority 2
assert h.heap[0][1] == 'a'   # a should be at root
k, p = h.pop()
assert k == 'a' and p == 2
k, p = h.pop()
assert k == 'b' and p == 5
print('PASS')
